    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 646
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/646](https://www.erdosproblems.com/646)
    23→
    24→Let $p_1, \ldots, p_k$ be distinct primes. Are there infinitely many $n$ such that
    25→$n!$ is divisible by an even power of each of the $p_i$?
    26→
    27→The answer is yes, proved by Berend [Be97], who further proved that the
    28→sequence of such $n$ has bounded gaps (where the bound depends on the initial
    29→set of primes).
    30→
    31→[Be97] Berend, D., *On the parity of exponents in the factorization of $n!$*. J. Number Theory **64** (1997), 13–19.
    32→-/
    33→
    34→namespace Erdos646
    35→
    36→/--
    37→Erdős Problem 646 (proved by Berend [Be97]):
    38→
    39→For any finite set of primes, there are infinitely many $n$ such that
    40→$n!$ is divisible by an even power of each prime in the set. Equivalently,
    41→for each prime $p$ in the set, the $p$-adic valuation of $n!$ is even.
    42→-/
    43→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    44→theorem erdos_646 : answer(True) ↔
    45→    ∀ (primes : Finset ℕ), (∀ p ∈ primes, Nat.Prime p) →
    46→    Set.Infinite {n : ℕ | ∀ p ∈ primes, Even (padicValNat p n.factorial)} := by
    47→  sorry
    48→
    49→end Erdos646
    50→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 23
    21→
    22→*References:*
    23→* [erdosproblems.com/23](https://www.erdosproblems.com/23)
    24→* [OEIS A389646](https://oeis.org/A389646)
    25→-/
    26→
    27→open SimpleGraph BigOperators Classical
    28→
    29→namespace Erdos23
    30→
    31→/--
    32→Every triangle-free graph on $5$ vertices can be made bipartite by removing at most $1$ edge.
    33→This is the $n = 1$ case of Erdős Problem 23.
    34→-/
    35→@[category test, AMS 5]
    36→theorem erdos_23_n1 :
    37→    ∀ (G : SimpleGraph (Fin 5)), G.CliqueFree 3 → ∃ (H : SimpleGraph (Fin 5)),
    38→        H ≤ G ∧ H.IsBipartite ∧ (G.edgeFinset \ H.edgeFinset).card ≤ 1 := by
    39→  sorry
    40→
    41→/--
    42→There exists a triangle-free graph on $5$ vertices such that at least $1$ edge must be removed
    43→to make it bipartite. This shows the bound in `erdos_23_n1` is tight.
    44→-/
    45→@[category test, AMS 5]
    46→theorem erdos_23_n1_tight :
    47→    ∃ (G : SimpleGraph (Fin 5)), G.CliqueFree 3 ∧ ∀ (H : SimpleGraph (Fin 5)),
    48→        H ≤ G → H.IsBipartite → 1 ≤ (G.edgeFinset \ H.edgeFinset).card := by
    49→  sorry
    50→
    51→/--
    52→The blow-up of the 5-cycle $C_5$: replace each vertex of $C_5$ with an independent set of $n$
    53→vertices, and connect two vertices iff their corresponding vertices in $C_5$ are adjacent.
    54→The vertex set is $\mathbb{Z}/5\mathbb{Z} \times \{0, \ldots, n-1\}$, where $(i, a)$ and $(j, b)$
    55→are adjacent iff $j = i + 1$ or $i = j + 1$ in $\mathbb{Z}/5\mathbb{Z}$.
    56→-/
    57→def blowupC5 (n : ℕ) : SimpleGraph (ZMod 5 × Fin n) :=
    58→  SimpleGraph.fromRel fun (i, _) (j, _) => i + 1 = j ∨ j + 1 = i
    59→
    60→/--
    61→The blow-up of $C_5$ shows that the bound $n^2$ in Erdős Problem 23 is tight:
    62→any bipartite subgraph must omit at least $n^2$ edges.
    63→-/
    64→@[category test, AMS 5]
    65→theorem blowupC5_tight (n : ℕ) (_hn : 0 < n) (H : SimpleGraph (ZMod 5 × Fin n))
    66→    (hH : H ≤ blowupC5 n) (hBip : H.IsBipartite) :
    67→    n ^ 2 ≤ ((blowupC5 n).edgeFinset \ H.edgeFinset).card := by
    68→  sorry
    69→
    70→/--
    71→Can every triangle-free graph on $5n$ vertices be made bipartite by deleting at most $n^2$ edges?
    72→-/
    73→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    74→theorem erdos_23 : answer(sorry) ↔
    75→    ∀ (n : ℕ) (V : Type) [Fintype V], Fintype.card V = 5 * n →
    76→      ∀ (G : SimpleGraph V), G.CliqueFree 3 →
    77→        ∃ (H : SimpleGraph V),
    78→          H ≤ G ∧ H.IsBipartite ∧ (G.edgeFinset \ H.edgeFinset).card ≤ n^2 := by
    79→  sorry
    80→
    81→-- TODO: add the remaining variants/statements/comments
    82→
    83→end Erdos23
    84→

    20→/-!

    22→
    23→*References:*
    24→ - [erdosproblems.com/252](https://www.erdosproblems.com/252)
    25→ - [ErSt71] Erdös, P., and E. G. Straus. "Some number theoretic results." Pacific J. Math 36 (1971):
    26→    635-646.
    27→ - [ErSt74] Erdős, Paul, and Ernst Straus. "On the irrationality of certain series." Pacific journal
    28→    of mathematics 55.1 (1974): 85-92.
    29→ - [ErKa54] P. Erdős, M. Kac, Amer. Math. Monthly 61 (1954), Problem 4518.
    30→ - [ScPu06] Schlage-Puchta, J. C., The irrationality of a number theoretical series. Ramanujan J.
    31→    (2006), 455-460.
    32→ - [FLC07] Friedlander, J. B. and Luca, F. and Stoiciu, M., On the irrationality of a divisor
    33→    function series. Integers (2007).
    34→ - [Pr22] Pratt, K., The irrationality of a divisor function series of Erdős and Kac.
    35→    arXiv:2209.11124 (2022).
    36→-/
    37→
    38→open scoped Nat ArithmeticFunction.sigma
    39→
    40→namespace Erdos252
    41→
    42→
    43→/-- The series `∑ σ k n / n!`. -/
    44→noncomputable def erdos_252_sum (k : ℕ) : ℝ := ∑' n, σ k n / (n ! : ℝ)
    45→
    46→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    47→theorem erdos_252 :
    48→    answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ k ≥ 1, Irrational (erdos_252_sum k) := by
    49→  sorry
    50→
    51→/-- `∑ σ 0 n / n!` is irrational. This is proved in [ErSt71]. -/
    52→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    53→theorem erdos_252.variants.k_eq_zero : Irrational (erdos_252_sum 0) := by
    54→  sorry
    55→
    56→/-- `∑ σ 1 n / n!` is irrational. This is proved in [ErSt74]. -/
    57→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    58→theorem erdos_252.variants.k_eq_one : Irrational (erdos_252_sum 1) := by
    59→  sorry
    60→
    61→
    62→/-- `∑ σ 2 n / n!` is irrational. This is proved in [ErKa54]. -/
    63→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    64→theorem erdos_252.variants.k_eq_two : Irrational (erdos_252_sum 2) := by
    65→  sorry
    66→
    67→/-- `∑ σ 3 n / n!` is irrational. This is proved in [ScPu06] and [FLC07]. -/
    68→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    69→theorem erdos_252.variants.k_eq_three : Irrational (erdos_252_sum 3) := by
    70→  sorry
    71→
    72→/-- `∑ σ 4 n / n!` is irrational. This is proved in [Pr22]. -/
    73→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    74→theorem erdos_252.variants.k_eq_four : Irrational (erdos_252_sum 4) := by
    75→  sorry
    76→
    77→/-- For a fixed `k ≥ 5`, is `∑ σ k n / n!` irrational?. -/
    78→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    79→theorem erdos_252.variants.k_ge_five :
    80→    answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ k ≥ 5, Irrational (erdos_252_sum k) := by
    81→  sorry
    82→
    83→/-- If Schinzel's conjecture is true, then `∑ σ k n / n!` is irrational for all `k`. This is proved
    84→in [ScPu06]. -/
    85→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    86→theorem erdos_252.variants.schinzel (hs : ∀ (fs : Finset (Polynomial ℤ)),
    87→    (∀ f ∈ fs, BunyakovskyCondition f) → SchinzelCondition fs →
    88→    Infinite ↑{n | ∀ f ∈ fs, Prime (Polynomial.eval (↑n) f).natAbs}) :
    89→    ∀ k, Irrational (erdos_252_sum k) := by
    90→  sorry
    91→
    92→/-- If the prime `k`-tuples conjecture is true, then `∑ σ k n / n!` is irrational. This is proved
    93→in [FLC07]. -/
    94→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    95→theorem erdos_252.variants.prime_tuples {k : ℕ} (hk : 4 ≤ k) (hp : ∀ (a : Fin k → ℕ+)
    96→    (b : Fin k → ℕ) (hab : ∀ p, p.Prime → ∃ n, ¬ p ∣ ∏ i, (a i * n + b i)),
    97→    Set.Infinite {n | ∀ i : Fin k, (a i * n + b i).Prime} ) :
    98→    Irrational (erdos_252_sum k) := by
    99→  sorry
   100→
   101→end Erdos252
   102→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 404
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/404](https://www.erdosproblems.com/404)
    23→
    24→For which integers $a \geq 1$ and primes $p$ is there a finite upper bound on those $k$
    25→such that there are $a = a_1 < \cdots < a_n$ with $p^k \mid (a_1! + \cdots + a_n!)$? If $f(a,p)$
    26→is the greatest such $k$, how does this function behave?
    27→
    28→Is there a prime $p$ and an infinite sequence $a_1 < a_2 < \cdots$ such that if $p^{m_k}$
    29→is the highest power of $p$ dividing $\sum_{i \leq k} a_i!$ then $m_k \to \infty$?
    30→
    31→See also Problem 403. Lin [Li76] has shown that $f(2,2) \leq 254$.
    32→
    33→[ErGr80] Erdős, P. and Graham, R., _Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number
    34→theory_. Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathematique (1980).
    35→
    36→[Li76] Lin, S., _Computer experiments on sequences which form integral bases_ (1976).
    37→-/
    38→
    39→open Filter
    40→
    41→open scoped BigOperators
    42→
    43→namespace Erdos404
    44→
    45→/--
    46→Erdős Problem 404, Part 1 [ErGr80, p.79]:
    47→
    48→For all integers $a \geq 1$ and all primes $p$, there is a finite upper bound on the
    49→$p$-adic valuation of sums $a_1! + \cdots + a_n!$ over all finite sets $\{a_1, \ldots, a_n\}$ with
    50→$a$ as the minimum element.
    51→
    52→Formally: for every $a \geq 1$ and every prime $p$, there exists $K$ such that for every
    53→finite set $S$ of natural numbers with $a \in S$ and $a \leq x$ for all $x \in S$, we have
    54→$v_p\!\left(\sum_{i \in S} i!\right) \leq K$.
    55→-/
    56→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    57→theorem erdos_404 (a : ℕ) (ha : 1 ≤ a) (p : ℕ) (hp : Nat.Prime p) :
    58→    ∃ K : ℕ, ∀ S : Finset ℕ, a ∈ S → (∀ x ∈ S, a ≤ x) →
    59→      padicValNat p (∑ i ∈ S, i.factorial) ≤ K := by
    60→  sorry
    61→
    62→/--
    63→Erdős Problem 404, Part 2 [ErGr80, p.79]:
    64→
    65→Is there a prime $p$ and strictly increasing sequence $a_1 < a_2 < \cdots$ of natural
    66→numbers such that the $p$-adic valuation of the partial sums $\sum_{i \leq k} a_i!$ tends
    67→to infinity?
    68→
    69→This is expected to be a consequence of Part 1: if $f(a,p)$ is always finite, then for any
    70→sequence starting at $a_1$, the $p$-adic valuation of partial sums is bounded by
    71→$f(a_1, p)$.
    72→-/
    73→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    74→theorem erdos_404.variants.divergence : answer(sorry) ↔
    75→    ∃ (p : ℕ) (_ : Nat.Prime p) (a : ℕ → ℕ), StrictMono a ∧

    77→        atTop atTop := by
    78→  sorry
    79→
    80→end Erdos404

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 933
    21→
    22→Erdős asked whether the limsup of the ratio of the {2,3}-smooth part of n(n+1)
    23→to n·log(n) is infinite.
    24→
    25→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/933](https://www.erdosproblems.com/933)
    26→
    27→[Er76d] Erdős, P., _Problems in number theory and combinatorics_ (1976).
    28→-/
    29→
    30→open Real
    31→
    32→namespace Erdos933
    33→
    34→/--
    35→The $\{2,3\}$-smooth part of a natural number $n$, i.e., $2^{v_2(n)} \cdot 3^{v_3(n)}$,
    36→where $v_p$ denotes the $p$-adic valuation.
    37→-/
    38→noncomputable def smoothPart23 (n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    39→  2 ^ padicValNat 2 n * 3 ^ padicValNat 3 n
    40→
    41→/--
    42→Erdős Problem 933 [Er76d]:
    43→
    44→If $n(n+1) = 2^k \cdot 3^l \cdot m$ where $\gcd(m, 6) = 1$, is it true that
    45→$$\limsup_{n \to \infty} \frac{2^k \cdot 3^l}{n \log n} = \infty?$$
    46→
    47→Equivalently: for every $C > 0$, there exist arbitrarily large $n$ such that
    48→the $\{2,3\}$-smooth part of $n(n+1)$ exceeds $C \cdot n \cdot \log n$.
    49→
    50→Mahler proved that $2^k \cdot 3^l < n^{1+o(1)}$.
    51→Erdős wrote that 'it is easy to see' that for infinitely many $n$,
    52→$2^k \cdot 3^l > n \cdot \log n$.
    53→-/
    54→@[category research open, AMS 11]
    55→theorem erdos_933 : answer(sorry) ↔
    56→    ∀ C : ℝ, C > 0 →
    57→    ∀ N : ℕ, ∃ n : ℕ, N ≤ n ∧
    58→      (smoothPart23 (n * (n + 1)) : ℝ) > C * (n : ℝ) * Real.log (n : ℝ) := by
    59→  sorry
    60→
    61→end Erdos933

    21→theorem prod_primeFactors_factorization_apply (n : ℕ) {p : ℕ} (hp : p.Prime) {f : ℕ → ℕ → ℕ}
    22→    (hf : ∀ q n, ¬ q ∣ n → f q n = 0) (hf₀ : ∀ q, f q 0 = 0):

    25→      rw [factorization_pow, (mem_primeFactors.1 hx).1.factorization]]

    17→import Mathlib.Algebra.Order.Ring.Nat

    21→namespace Nat
    22→
    23→/--
    24→A natural number $n$ is said to be $k$-full (or $k$-powerful) if for every prime factor $p$ of $n$,
    25→the $k$-th power $p^k$ also divides $n$.
    26→-/
    27→def Full (k : ℕ) (n : ℕ) : Prop := ∀ p ∈ n.primeFactors, p^k ∣ n
    28→
    29→instance Full.decide : ∀ k n, Decidable (Full k n) := by
    30→  intro k n
    31→  dsimp [Full]
    32→  infer_instance
    33→
    34→/-- Every natural number is $0$-full. -/
    35→theorem Full.zero_left (n : ℕ) : (0 : ℕ).Full n := by
    36→  simp [Nat.Full]
    37→
    38→/-- Every natural number is $1$-full. -/
    39→theorem Full.one_left (n : ℕ) : (1 : ℕ).Full n := by
    40→  aesop (add simp [Nat.Full])
    41→
    42→/-- $0$ is always $k$-full. -/
    43→theorem Full.zero_right (k : ℕ) : k.Full 0 := by
    44→  simp [Nat.Full]
    45→
    46→/-- $1$ is always $k$-full. -/
    47→theorem Full.one_right (k : ℕ) : k.Full 1 := by
    48→  simp [Nat.Full]
    49→
    50→/--
    51→A [Powerful number](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Powerful_number) is a natural number $n$ where
    52→for every prime divisor $p$, $p^2$ divides $n$.
    53→Powerful numbers are also known as "squareful", "square-full", or "$2$-full".
    54→-/
    55→abbrev Powerful : ℕ → Prop := (2).Full
    56→
    57→instance Powerful.decide : ∀ n, Decidable (Powerful n) := by
    58→  intro n
    59→  dsimp [Powerful, Full]
    60→  apply Finset.decidableDforallFinset
    61→
    62→theorem full_of_le_full (k : ℕ) (n : ℕ) {m : ℕ} (hk : k ≤ m) (h : m.Full n) : k.Full n :=
    63→  fun p a ↦ pow_dvd_of_le_of_pow_dvd hk (h p a)
    64→
    65→/-- If $n \equiv p \pmod{p ^ (k + 1)}$, for a prime $p$ then $n$ is not $(k + 1)$-full. -/
    66→theorem not_full_of_prime_mod_prime_sq (n : ℕ) (k : ℕ) {p : ℕ} (hp : p.Prime)
    67→    (h : n % p ^ (k + 1) = p) : ¬ (k + 1).Full n := by
    68→  rw [Full]
    69→  push_neg
    70→  use p
    71→  simp [mem_primeFactors, hp, ne_eq, true_and]
    72→  constructor
    73→  · rw [←Nat.div_add_mod n (p ^ (k + 1)), h]
    74→    have : p ∣ p ^ (k + 1) := by exact Dvd.intro_left (p.pow k) rfl
    75→    simp
    76→    exact ⟨Dvd.dvd.mul_right this (n / p ^ (k + 1)), fun a ↦ Prime.ne_zero hp⟩
    77→  · intro h
    78→    simp_all [Nat.dvd_iff_mod_eq_zero.mp h]
    79→    aesop

    82→open Lean Meta Qq in
    83→/-- Simproc to compute the set `Nat.primeFactors`. -/
    84→dsimproc primeFactorsEq (Nat.primeFactors _) := fun e ↦ do
    85→  unless e.isAppOfArity `Nat.primeFactors 1 do return .continue
    86→  let some n ← fromExpr? e.appArg! | return .continue
    87→  let outAsList : List Q(ℕ) := (unsafe n.primeFactors.val.unquot).map mkNatLit
    88→  let outAsFinset : Q(Finset ℕ) := outAsList.foldl (fun s n ↦ q(insert $n $s)) q({})
    89→  return .done outAsFinset

    19→/-!
    20→# Beaver Math Olympiad (BMO)
    21→
    22→The Beaver Math Olympiad (BMO) is a set of mathematical reformulations of the halting/nonhalting
    23→problem of specific Turing machines from all-0 tape. These problems came from studying small Busy
    24→Beaver values. Some problems are open and have a conjectured answer, some are open and don't have a
    25→conjectured answer, and, some are solved.
    26→
    27→Among these problems is the Collatz-like *Antihydra* problem which is open and coming from a 6-state
    28→Turing machine, and a testament to the difficulty of knowing the sixth Busy Beaver value.
    29→
    30→For some BMO problem, the equivalence between the mathematical formulation and the corresponding
    31→Turing machine non-termination has been formally proved in Rocq, we indicate it when done.
    32→
    33→*References:*
    34→
    35→- [bbchallenge.org](https://bbchallenge.org)
    36→- [Beaver Math Olympiad wiki page](https://wiki.bbchallenge.org/wiki/Beaver_Math_Olympiad)
    37→- [Antihydra web page](https://bbchallenge.org/antihydra)
    38→- [Antihydra wiki page](https://wiki.bbchallenge.org/wiki/Antihydra)
    39→-/
    40→
    41→namespace BeaverMathOlympiad
    42→
    43→/--
    44→[BMO#1](https://wiki.bbchallenge.org/wiki/Beaver_Math_Olympiad#1._1RB1RE_1LC0RA_0RD1LB_---1RC_1LF1RE_0LB0LE_(bbch))
    45→
    46→Let $(a_n)_{n \ge 1}$ and $(b_n)_{n \ge 1}$ be two sequences such that $(a_1, b_1) = (1, 2)$ and
    47→
    48→$$(a_{n+1}, b_{n+1}) = \begin{cases}
    49→(a_n-b_n, 4b_n+2) & \text{if }a_n \ge b_n \\
    50→(2a_n+1, b_n-a_n) & \text{if }a_n < b_n
    51→\end{cases}$$
    52→
    53→for all positive integers $n$. Does there exist a positive integer $i$ such that $a_i = b_i$?
    54→
    55→The first 10 values of $(a_n, b_n)$ are $(1, 2), (3, 1), (2, 6), (5, 4), (1, 18), (3, 17),
    56→(7, 14), (15, 7), (8, 30), (17, 22)$.
    57→
    58→[BMO#1](https://wiki.bbchallenge.org/wiki/Beaver_Math_Olympiad#1._1RB1RE_1LC0RA_0RD1LB_---1RC_1LF1RE_0LB0LE_(bbch)) is equivalent to asking whether the 6-state Turing machine
    59→[`1RB1RE_1LC0RA_0RD1LB_---1RC_1LF1RE_0LB0LE`](https://wiki.bbchallenge.org/wiki/1RB1RE_1LC0RA_0RD1LB_---1RC_1LF1RE_0LB0LE) halts or not.
    60→
    61→There is presently no consensus on whether the machine halts or not, hence the problem is formulated
    62→using `answer(sorry) ↔`.
    63→
    64→The machine was discovered by [bbchallenge.org](bbchallenge.org) contributor Jason Yuen on
    65→June 25th 2024.
    66→-/
    67→@[category research open, AMS 5 11 68]
    68→theorem beaver_math_olympiad_problem_1 :
    69→    answer(sorry) ↔ ∀ᵉ (a : ℕ → ℕ) (b : ℕ → ℕ)
    70→    (a_ini : a 0 = 1)
    71→    (a_rec : ∀ n, a (n + 1) = if b n ≤ a n then a n - b n else 2 * a n + 1)
    72→    (b_ini : b 0 = 2)
    73→    (b_rec : ∀ n, b (n + 1) = if b n ≤ a n then 4 * b n + 2 else b n - a n),
    74→    ∃ i, a i = b i := by
    75→  sorry
    76→
    77→/--
    78→[BMO#2](https://wiki.bbchallenge.org/wiki/Beaver_Math_Olympiad#2._Hydra_and_Antihydra)
    79→
    80→Antihydra is a sequence starting at 8, and iterating the function
    81→$$H(n) = \left\lfloor \frac {3n}2 \right\rfloor.$$
    82→The conjecture states that the cumulative number of odd values in this sequence
    83→is never more than twice the cumulative number of even values. It is a relatively new open problem
    84→with, so it might be solvable, although seems quite hard because of its Collatz-like flavor.
    85→The underlying Collatz-like map has been studied independently in the past,
    86→see doi:[10.1017/S0017089508004655](https://doi.org/10.1017/S0017089508004655) (Corollary 4).
    87→
    88→It is equivalent to non-termination of the [`1RB1RA_0LC1LE_1LD1LC_1LA0LB_1LF1RE_---0RA`](https://wiki.bbchallenge.org/wiki/Antihydra) 6-state Turing machine (from all-0 tape). Note that the conjecture
    89→that the machine does not halt is based on [a probabilistic argument](https://wiki.bbchallenge.org/wiki/Antihydra#Trajectory).
    90→
    91→This machine and its mathematical reformulations were found by [bbchallenge.org](bbchallenge.org)
    92→contributors mxdys and Rachel Hunter on June 28th 2024.
    93→-/
    94→@[category research open, AMS 5 11 68]
    95→theorem beaver_math_olympiad_problem_2_antihydra
    96→    (a : ℕ → ℕ) (b : ℕ → ℤ)
    97→    (a_ini : a 0 = 8)
    98→    (a_rec : ∀ n, a (n + 1) = (3 * a n) / 2)
    99→    (b_ini : b 0 = 0)
   100→    (b_rec : ∀ n, b (n + 1) = if a n % 2 = 0 then b n + 2 else b n - 1) :
   101→    ∀ n, b n ≥ 0 := by
   102→  sorry
   103→
   104→/--
   105→[BMO#2](https://wiki.bbchallenge.org/wiki/Beaver_Math_Olympiad#2._Hydra_and_Antihydra) formulation variant
   106→
   107→Alternative statement of beaver_math_olympiad_problem_2_antihydra
   108→using set size comparison instead of a recurrent sequence b.
   109→-/
   110→@[category research open, AMS 5 11 68]
   111→theorem beaver_math_olympiad_problem_2_antihydra.variants.set

   113→    (a_rec : ∀ n, a (n + 1) = (3 * a n) / 2) (n : ℕ) :
   114→    ((Finset.Ico 0 n).filter fun x ↦ Odd (a x)).card ≤
   115→      2 * ((Finset.Ico 0 n).filter fun x ↦ Even (a x)).card := by
   116→  sorry
   117→
   118→/--
   119→[BMO#3][https://wiki.bbchallenge.org/wiki/Beaver_Math_Olympiad#3._1RB0RB3LA4LA2RA_2LB3RA---3RA4RB_(bbch)_and_1RB1RB3LA4LA2RA_2LB3RA---3RA4RB_(bbch)]
   120→
   121→Let $v_2(n)$ be the largest integer $k$ such that $2^k$ divides $n$.
   122→Let $(a_n)_{n \ge 0}$ be a sequence such that
   123→
   124→$$a_n = \begin{cases}
   125→2 & \text{if } n=0 \\
   126→a_{n-1}+2^{v_2(a_{n-1})+2}-1 & \text{if } n \ge 1
   127→\end{cases}$$
   128→
   129→for all non-negative integers $n$. Is there an integer $n$ such that $a_n=4^k$ for
   130→some positive integer $k$?
   131→
   132→[BMO#3][https://wiki.bbchallenge.org/wiki/Beaver_Math_Olympiad#3._1RB0RB3LA4LA2RA_2LB3RA---3RA4RB_(bbch)_and_1RB1RB3LA4LA2RA_2LB3RA---3RA4RB_(bbch)] is equivalent to the non-termination of 2-state 5-symbol Turing machine [`1RB0RB3LA4LA2RA_2LB3RA---3RA4RB`](https://wiki.bbchallenge.org/wiki/1RB0RB3LA4LA2RA_2LB3RA---3RA4RB) (from all-0 tape).
   133→
   134→The machine was found and informally proven not to halt by [bbchallenge.org](bbchallenge.org)
   135→contributor Daniel Yuan on June 18th 2024; see [Discord discussion](https://discord.com/channels/960643023006490684/1084047886494470185/1252634913220591728).
   136→-/
   137→@[category research solved, AMS 5 11 68]
   138→theorem beaver_math_olympiad_problem_3
   139→    (a : ℕ → ℕ)
   140→    (a_ini : a 0 = 2)
   141→    (a_rec : ∀ n, a (n + 1) = (a n) + 2 ^ ((padicValNat 2 (a n)) + 2) - 1) :
   142→    ¬ (∃ n k, a n = 4 ^ k) := by
   143→  sorry
   144→
   145→/--
   146→[BMO#4](https://wiki.bbchallenge.org/wiki/Beaver_Math_Olympiad#4._1RB3RB---1LB0LA_2LA4RA3LA4RB1LB_(bbch))
   147→
   148→Bonnie the beaver was bored, so she tried to construct a sequence of integers $\{a_n\}_{n \ge 0}$.
   149→She first defined $a_0=2$, then defined $a_{n+1}$ depending on $a_n$ and $n$
   150→using the following rules:

    16→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    17→
    18→/-!
    19→$a(n)$ is the minimum integer $k$ such that the smallest prime factor of the
    20→$n$-th Fermat number exceeds $2^(2^n - k)$.
    21→
    22→*References:*
    23→- [A358684](https://oeis.org/A358684)
    24→- [SA22](https://doi.org/10.26493/2590-9770.1473.ec5) Lorenzo Sauras-Altuzarra, *Some properties of the factors of Fermat numbers*, Art Discrete Appl. Math. (2022).
    25→-/
    26→
    27→
    28→namespace OeisA358684
    29→
    30→open Nat
    31→
    32→/--
    33→A358684: $a(n)$ is the minimum integer $k$ such that the smallest prime factor of the $n$-th Fermat
    34→number exceeds $2^{2^n - k}$.
    35→Let $F_n = 2^{2^n} + 1$ be the $n$-th Fermat number, and $P_n$ be its smallest prime factor.
    36→The definition of $a(n)$ is equivalent to the closed form:
    37→$$a(n) = 2^n - \lfloor \log_2(P_n) \rfloor$$
    38→where $P_n = \operatorname{minFac}(\operatorname{fermatNumber} n)$.
    39→The subtraction is defined in $\mathbb{N}$ and is safe
    40→ since $P_n \le F_n$, implying $\log_2 P_n < 2^n$.
    41→-/
    42→def a (n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    43→  letI pn := minFac (fermatNumber n)
    44→  (2 ^ n) - (log2 pn)
    45→
    46→/--
    47→The "original" definition: $a'(n)$ is the minimum $k$ such that $P_n > 2^{2^n - k}$.
    48→We use `Nat.find` which returns the smallest natural number satisfying a predicate.
    49→-/
    50→noncomputable def a' (n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    51→  letI Pn := minFac (fermatNumber n)
    52→  Nat.find (show ∃ k, Pn > 2 ^ (2 ^ n - k) from by
    53→    use 2^n
    54→    simp only [tsub_self, pow_zero]
    55→    simp [Pn]
    56→    unfold Nat.fermatNumber
    57→    exact (Nat.minFac_prime (by norm_num)).one_lt
    58→  )
    59→
    60→
    61→/--
    62→The log2 of the smallest prime factor of $F_n$ is at most $2^n$.
    63→-/
    64→@[category undergraduate, AMS 11]
    65→private lemma log2_minFac_le (n : ℕ) : log2 (fermatNumber n).minFac ≤ 2^n := by
    66→  rw [log2_eq_log_two]
    67→  refine (log_mono_right (minFac_le (by rw [fermatNumber]; norm_num))).trans_eq ?_
    68→  rw [fermatNumber, log_eq_of_pow_le_of_lt_pow (le_add_right _ _)]
    69→  rw [pow_succ, mul_two]
    70→  gcongr
    71→  exact one_lt_pow (by norm_num) (by norm_num)
    72→
    73→/--
    74→The minimization definition is equivalent to the closed form.
    75→-/
    76→@[category API, AMS 11]
    77→theorem a_equiv_a' (n : ℕ) : a n = a' n := by
    78→  unfold a a'; set Pn := (fermatNumber n).minFac
    79→  rw [Eq.comm, Nat.find_eq_iff]
    80→  constructor
    81→  · rw [tsub_tsub_cancel_of_le (log2_minFac_le n), log2_eq_log_two]
    82→    refine lt_of_le_of_ne (pow_log_le_self 2 (Nat.Prime.ne_zero (minFac_prime ?_))) fun h => ?_
    83→    · rw [fermatNumber]
    84→      norm_num
    85→    · have hPn : Pn.Prime := minFac_prime (by rw [fermatNumber]; norm_num)
    86→      have hnz := log2_eq_log_two ▸ (Nat.log_pos one_lt_two hPn.two_le).ne'
    87→      refine (Nat.not_even_iff_odd.mpr <| (odd_pow_iff hnz).mp ?_) even_two
    88→      rw [log2_eq_log_two, h]
    89→      exact Odd.of_dvd_nat (odd_fermatNumber n) (minFac_dvd _)
    90→  · intro m hm; simp only [not_lt]
    91→    rw [lt_tsub_iff_left] at hm
    92→    rw [log2_eq_log_two] at hm
    93→    refine (lt_pow_succ_log_self (b:=2) (by norm_num) _).le.trans ?_
    94→    apply Nat.pow_le_pow_right (by norm_num)
    95→    apply le_tsub_of_add_le_right
    96→    have := succ_le_of_lt hm
    97→    omega
    98→
    99→@[category test, AMS 11]
   100→theorem zero : a 0 = 0 := by norm_num [a]; simp [log2_def]
   101→
   102→@[category test, AMS 11]
   103→theorem one : a 1 = 0 := by norm_num [a]; simp [log2_def]
   104→
   105→@[category test, AMS 11]
   106→theorem two : a 2 = 0 := by norm_num [a]; simp [log2_def]
   107→
   108→@[category test, AMS 11]
   109→theorem three : a 3 = 0 := by
   110→  norm_num only [a, Nat.log2_eq_log_two,Nat.fermatNumber]
   111→
   112→@[category test, AMS 11]
   113→theorem four : a 0 = 0 := by norm_num [a]; simp [log2_def]
   114→
   115→@[category test, AMS 11]
   116→theorem five : a 5 = 23 := by
   117→  norm_num [a, fermatNumber,Nat.log2_eq_log_two]
   118→
   119→@[category test, AMS 11]
   120→theorem six : a 6 = 46 := by
   121→  -- AlphaProof failed to close this goal
   122→  sorry
   123→
   124→@[category test, AMS 11]
   125→theorem seven : a 7 = 73 := by
   126→  sorry
   127→
   128→/--
   129→Conjecture: the dyadic valuation of A93179(n) - 1 does not exceed 2^n - a(n).
   130→
   131→A93179(n) is minFac(fermatNumber n), the smallest prime factor of the n-th Fermat number.
   132→The conjecture states that if $P_n$ is the smallest prime factor of the $n$-th Fermat number,
   133→then $\nu_2(P_n - 1) \le 2^n - a(n)$.
   134→Substituting the definition of $a(n)$, this is equivalent to $\nu_2(P_n - 1) \le \lfloor \log_2(P_n) \rfloor$.
   135→
   136→This is Conjecture 3.4 in [SA22].
   137→-/
   138→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
   139→theorem oeis_358684_conjecture_0 (n : ℕ) :
   140→    padicValNat 2 (minFac (fermatNumber n) - 1) ≤ 2 ^ n - a n := by
   141→  delta fermatNumber and a
   142→  rw [Nat.sub_sub_self]
   143→  · rw [Nat.log2_eq_log_two]
   144→    apply Nat.le_log_of_pow_le (by decide)
   145→    refine le_trans ?_ <| sub_le _ 1
   146→    apply Nat.ordProj_le 2
   147→    exact Nat.sub_ne_zero_of_lt (Nat.minFac_prime (by norm_num)).one_lt
   148→  · rw [Nat.log2_eq_log_two]
   149→    have : (2 ^ 2 ^ n) + 1 < 2 ^ ((2 ^ n) + 1) := by
   150→      simp [pow_add, mul_two]

/-- Erdős' construction: all integers in $[1, \sqrt{N/2}]$ together with all
even integers in $(\sqrt{N/2}, \sqrt{2N}]$. -/
noncomputable def erdosConstruction (N : ℕ) : Finset ℕ :=
  let lo := Nat.sqrt (N / 2)
  let hi := Nat.sqrt (2 * N)
  (Finset.Icc 1 lo) ∪ ((Finset.Ioc lo hi).filter (fun m => Even m))

/-- Erdős Problem 441, asymptotic upper bound (proved by Chen [Ch98]):
$g(N) \sim (9N/8)^{1/2}$. For any $\varepsilon > 0$ and sufficiently large $N$, any
$A \subseteq \{1, \ldots, N\}$ with all pairwise $\operatorname{lcm} \leq N$ satisfies
$|A| \leq (1+\varepsilon) \sqrt{9N/8}$. -/

namespace Erdos844

/-- The candidate extremal set for Erdős Problem 844: all numbers in
$\{1, \ldots, N\}$ that are either even or not squarefree. -/
noncomputable def extremalSet (N : ℕ) : Finset ℕ :=
  (Finset.Icc 1 N).filter (fun n => Even n ∨ ¬Squarefree n)

/--

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 235
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/235](https://www.erdosproblems.com/235)
    23→
    24→Let $N_k = p_1 \cdots p_k$ be the $k$-th primorial and let $a_1 < \cdots < a_{\varphi(N_k)}$
    25→be the integers less than $N_k$ coprime to $N_k$. Erdős conjectured that the fraction of
    26→consecutive gaps $a_{i+1} - a_i \le c \cdot N_k / \varphi(N_k)$ tends to a limit that is a
    27→continuous function of $c$. This was proved by Hooley.
    28→
    29→[Er55c] Erdős, P., _Some problems on number theory_ (1955).
    30→
    31→[Ho65] Hooley, C., _On the difference of consecutive numbers prime to n_, Acta Arith. 8 (1963),
    32→343-347.
    33→-/
    34→
    35→open Filter Nat Classical
    36→
    37→open scoped BigOperators
    38→
    39→namespace Erdos235
    40→
    41→/-- The $k$-th primorial: product of the first $k$ primes ($p_1 \cdot p_2 \cdots p_k$). -/
    42→noncomputable def primorial (k : ℕ) : ℕ := ∏ i ∈ Finset.range k, nth Nat.Prime i
    43→
    44→/-- The sorted list of natural numbers less than $n$ that are coprime to $n$. -/
    45→def sortedCoprimes (n : ℕ) : List ℕ :=
    46→  ((Finset.range n).filter (fun a => Nat.Coprime a n)).sort (· ≤ ·)
    47→
    48→/-- Count consecutive gaps $\le$ threshold in a sorted list of naturals. -/
    49→noncomputable def countSmallGaps : List ℕ → ℝ → ℕ
    50→  | [], _ => 0
    51→  | [_], _ => 0
    52→  | a :: b :: rest, threshold =>
    53→    (if ((b : ℝ) - (a : ℝ)) ≤ threshold then 1 else 0) + countSmallGaps (b :: rest) threshold
    54→
    55→/-- The fraction of consecutive gaps among reduced residues $\bmod N_k$
    56→that are at most $c \cdot N_k / \varphi(N_k)$. -/
    57→noncomputable def gapFraction (k : ℕ) (c : ℝ) : ℝ :=
    58→  let Nk := primorial k
    59→  let residues := sortedCoprimes Nk
    60→  let threshold := c * (Nk : ℝ) / (Nat.totient Nk : ℝ)
    61→  (countSmallGaps residues threshold : ℝ) / (Nat.totient Nk : ℝ)
    62→
    63→/--
    64→Erdős Problem 235 [Er55c] (proved by Hooley [Ho65]):
    65→
    66→Let $N_k = 2 \cdot 3 \cdots p_k$ (the $k$-th primorial) and let
    67→$a_1 < a_2 < \cdots < a_{\varphi(N_k)}$ be the integers less than $N_k$ coprime to $N_k$.
    68→Then for any $c \ge 0$, the limit
    69→$$
    70→\lim_{k \to \infty} \frac{\#\{i : a_i - a_{i-1} \le c \cdot N_k / \varphi(N_k)\}}{\varphi(N_k)}
    71→$$
    72→exists and defines a continuous function of $c$.
    73→
    74→Hooley proved that the limiting distribution is exponential: $f(c) = 1 - e^{-c}$.
    75→-/
    76→@[category research solved, AMS 11]
    77→theorem erdos_235 :
    78→    ∃ f : ℝ → ℝ, Continuous f ∧
    79→      ∀ c : ℝ, 0 ≤ c →
    80→        Tendsto (fun k => gapFraction k c) atTop (nhds (f c)) := by
    81→  sorry
    82→
    83→end Erdos235
    84→

    19→/-!
    20→# Wall-Sun-Sun primes
    21→
    22→*References:*
    23→- [Wikipedia, Lucas_sequence](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lucas_sequence)
    24→- [Wikipedia, Wall–Sun–Sun prime](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wall%E2%80%93Sun%E2%80%93Sun_prime)
    25→- [Wikipedia, Wieferich prime](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lucas%E2%80%93Wieferich_prime)
    26→-/
    27→
    28→open scoped NumberTheorySymbols
    29→
    30→/--
    31→**The Lucas sequence of the first kind**
    32→$U_0(P, Q) = 0$, $U_1(P, Q)=1$, $U_{n+2}(P, Q)=PU_{n+1}(P, Q)-QU_n(P, Q)$
    33→-/
    34→def LucasSequence.U (P Q : ℤ) : ℕ → ℤ
    35→  | 0 => 0
    36→  | 1 => 1
    37→  | n + 2 => P * LucasSequence.U P Q (n + 1) - Q * LucasSequence.U P Q n
    38→
    39→/--
    40→**The Lucas sequence of the second kind**
    41→$V_0(P, Q) = 0$, $V_1(P, Q)=P$, $V_{n+2}(P, Q)=PV_{n+1}(P, Q)-QV_n(P, Q)$
    42→-/
    43→def LucasSequence.V (P Q : ℤ) : ℕ → ℤ
    44→  | 0 => 2
    45→  | 1 => P
    46→  | n + 2 => P * LucasSequence.V P Q (n + 1) - Q * LucasSequence.V P Q n
    47→
    48→/--
    49→**The Lucas numbers**
    50→$L_0 = 2$, $L_1=1$, $L_{n+2} = L_{n+1}+L_n$
    51→-/
    52→def lucasNumber : ℕ → ℤ := LucasSequence.V 1 (-1)
    53→
    54→/--
    55→**Wall–Sun–Sun prime**
    56→A prime $p$ is a Wall–Sun–Sun prime if and only if $L_p \equiv 1 \pmod{p^2}$, where $L_p$ is
    57→the $p$-th Lucas number.
    58→-/
    59→structure IsWallSunSunPrime (p : ℕ) : Prop where
    60→  prime : p.Prime
    61→  lucasNumber_modeq : lucasNumber p ≡ 1 [ZMOD (p ^ 2)]
    62→
    63→/--
    64→**Lucas–Wieferich prime**
    65→A Lucas–Wieferich prime associated with $(a,b)$ is an odd prime $p$, not dividing $a^2 - 4b$, such
    66→that $U_{p-\varepsilon}(a,b) \equiv 0 \pmod{p^2}$ where $U(a,b)$ is the Lucas sequence of the first
    67→kind and $\varepsilon$ is the Legendre symbol $\left({\tfrac {a^{2}-4b}{p}}\right)$.
    68→
    69→See first paragraph of
    70→https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wall%E2%80%93Sun%E2%80%93Sun_prime#Wall%E2%80%93Sun%E2%80%93Sun_primes_with_discriminant_D
    71→for the condition that $p$ is odd and coprime to the discriminant
    72→-/
    73→structure IsLucasWieferichPrime (a b p : ℕ) : Prop where
    74→  prime : p.Prime
    75→  odd : Odd p
    76→  not_dvd : ¬(p : ℤ) ∣ a ^ 2 - 4 * b
    77→  modeq : LucasSequence.U a b (p - J(a^2 - 4*b | p)).toNat ≡ 0 [ZMOD (p^2)]
    78→
